tcp.conn_bytes_limit: Despite the name, this counts PACKETS, not bytes. b4 only sends the first N packets of each TCP connection through userspace inspection (the handshake/early-payload window); the rest stay on the kernel fast-path. Default 19, hard max 100. The cap is enforced at the nftables level via 'ct original packets < N+1'. Higher values let b4 apply DPI bypass to longer-lived flows but cost CPU and add latency to every connection. Lower values shrink the inspection window and may miss late TLS-handshake patterns the bypass logic depends on.
tcp.seg2delay: Milliseconds b4 waits AFTER sending segment 1 (first part of a split) BEFORE sending segment 2 (the rest). Used by the dominant two-segment splits: extsplit, firstbyte, tls, and DNS fragmentation. Defeats DPI middleboxes that read both segments within a single read window and reassemble them into one logical record before inspecting. The min/max range form randomizes the wait to evade timing fingerprints. In disorder mode (multi-segment splits) the same value is reused as the gap between every consecutive segment pair. Cost: every wait adds to TLS-handshake latency. Setting min and max to 0 disables the wait (fastest, but some DPI engines reassemble split segments anyway and the bypass loses effect).
tcp.dport_filter: Comma-separated list of TCP destination ports and ranges this set targets. Examples: '443', '80,443,5222', '1000-2000', or combinations. Empty means the set has no port-list contribution; b4 always queues port 443 by default regardless of any set's filter, so an empty filter still gets you 443. Each enabled set's DPortFilter values are unioned into the nftables 'tcp dport {...}' queue rule, so listed ports are sent to b4 userspace and unlisted ports stay on the kernel fast-path entirely. Used both for kernel-level filtering AND userspace per-set matching.
tcp.drop_sack: Strips the TCP SACK option (kind 5) AND the SACK-Permitted option (kind 4) from outgoing packets b4 reinjects. SACK-Permitted lives in the SYN, so removing it prevents the connection from negotiating SACK at all — both endpoints fall back to plain ACKs. Used to neutralize DPI that fingerprints clients by SACK behavior or treats SACK negotiation as a 'real client' signal. Side effect: throughput suffers on lossy links because retransmits become full-window resends instead of selective. Only applied when b4 is already reinjecting the packet for other reasons (fragmentation, faking, desync, etc.); has no effect on packets that pass through unmodified.
tcp.duplicate: Targeted feature: sends multiple copies of each matching packet to specific destination IPs (configured via the duplicate IP set, not all traffic). 'count' is how many copies are sent, range 1-10, default 3. Helps against DPI that probabilistically drops packets — extra copies improve the odds at least one survives the path. Costs: bandwidth multiplier on the destination, and some servers reject what looks like duplicate-segment retransmits. Only takes effect on packets going to the configured duplicate IP set; enabling this without populating the IP set changes nothing.
tcp.ip_block_detect: Detects when a destination is being silently IP-blocked by ISP-level DPI (the connection just hangs because nothing comes back) and fails it fast on the local side. Without this, the user's browser spins for 30+ seconds before giving up; with it, the user gets an immediate connection error and can retry or take a different route. If cache_blocked_ips is set, b4 remembers blocked destinations and fails subsequent connections to them immediately rather than waiting for the same evidence to accumulate again. Also feeds the Escalate.To chain — if the set has an escalation target, the blocked host is moved to that next set's bypass strategy instead of (or in addition to) failing fast. Watches HTTPS connections only.
tcp.ip_block_detect.retransmit_threshold: Sensitivity knob for ip_block_detect. Default 3, range 1-10. Lower values make b4 declare blocks faster (better responsiveness, more false positives on lossy links where retransmits are normal). Higher values are more tolerant (fewer false positives but the user waits longer before getting a fast-fail or strategy escalation). Threshold of 1 is dangerous — a single dropped packet on the path is enough to trigger a full block. Each connection is counted independently, so unrelated traffic doesn't influence detection on a different destination.
tcp.rst_protection: Drops incoming RST packets that look like middlebox injections rather than legitimate server resets. Without this, a single injected RST closes the connection and the bypass attempt fails. b4 distinguishes injected RSTs from legitimate ones using several signals derived from the RST and the connection's prior state; if the signals point to injection, the RST is dropped and the connection survives. Subsequent legitimate RSTs from the real server still close the connection normally. Also feeds the Escalate chain: when enough RSTs are dropped within Escalate.RstWindowSec, the host is escalated to the next set's bypass strategy.
tcp.rst_protection.ttl_tolerance: Tolerance knob for one of the rst_protection signals (TTL-based). Default 3, range 1-20. Lower = stricter detection of injected RSTs (more middlebox RSTs caught, but also a higher chance of dropping legitimate RSTs after a route change). Higher = more lenient (fewer false drops of legitimate RSTs, but more middlebox RSTs slip through). 0 is risky because routine network events like ECMP or a route flap can shift TTL by 1 hop. The other rst_protection signals continue to apply regardless of this value, so loosening tolerance doesn't disable RST protection entirely.
fragmentation.strategy: Selects the TCP-level bypass technique b4 applies to matching outgoing packets. Options: tcp, ip, oob, tls, disorder, extsplit, firstbyte, combo, hybrid, or none. Each is a different way of breaking up the TLS ClientHello so DPI middleboxes can't match it as a single record. 'none' disables fragmentation for this set — packets pass through unmodified. Choosing the right strategy is ISP-dependent: what defeats one DPI engine fails on another. The discovery feature (separate b4 tab) tests combinations against the user's network and recommends one. When fragmentation.strategy_pool is non-empty, this fixed selection is ignored and the pool is sampled per packet instead.
fragmentation.reverse_order: For two-segment strategies (extsplit, firstbyte, tls, DNS fragmentation), sends segment 2 before segment 1 instead of the natural order. TCP sequence numbers correctly identify which segment is which, so the destination reassembles fine. Some DPI middleboxes refuse out-of-order segments rather than reassembling, which means a reversed order forces them to give up. Other DPI engines reassemble correctly regardless of order, in which case this toggle has no effect. Does not apply to multi-segment strategies (disorder, combo) which already reorder internally.
fragmentation.strategy_pool: List of strategies to randomly sample from on each matched packet. When non-empty, OVERRIDES fragmentation.strategy entirely — every packet picks a strategy uniformly at random from the pool. Useful when no single strategy works consistently, or when DPI fingerprints clients by repeated-strategy patterns and a random-per-packet selection avoids that fingerprint. Costs: harder to diagnose which strategy is actually helping (logs only show what was rolled for each packet), and a randomly-picked wrong strategy can produce intermittent failures. Empty (default) means use the single fixed strategy from the strategy field.
fragmentation.oob_position: Numeric position used by the 'oob' fragmentation strategy when constructing its modified packet. The min/max range form randomizes the value per packet to avoid a fixed pattern. 0 falls back to a strategy-internal default. Has no effect when the active strategy is anything other than 'oob' — including when 'oob' appears only inside the strategy_pool, in which case it's used only on packets the pool happens to roll. Tuning is ISP-dependent; the discovery feature finds working values automatically and manual tuning is rarely needed.
fragmentation.tlsrec_pos: Numeric position used by the 'tls' fragmentation strategy to split a single TLS record into two well-formed TLS records inside the ClientHello. Different from a TCP segment split: the TLS-layer framing itself is rewritten so the DPI engine sees two valid TLS records instead of one. The min/max range form randomizes the position per packet. 0 falls back to a strategy-internal default. Has no effect when the active strategy is not 'tls'. The discovery feature finds working values automatically; manual tuning is rarely needed.
fragmentation.combo.first_byte_split: One of the split-point options the 'combo' strategy can combine. When enabled, b4 inserts a split very early in the TCP payload, so the first byte of the TLS ClientHello travels alone before the rest. Designed to be combined with combo's other split options — using it alone is rarely effective. Has no effect outside the combo strategy (including when 'combo' appears only in fragmentation.strategy_pool, in which case it's used only on packets the pool happens to roll). Cost: an extra small TCP segment per matched connection — negligible bandwidth, marginal latency.
fragmentation.combo.extension_split: One of the split-point options the 'combo' strategy can combine. When enabled, b4 looks for a clean boundary inside the ClientHello's TLS extensions and inserts a split there, so the first portion of the extensions ends one segment and the rest (including SNI) begins another. Has no effect outside the combo strategy. May silently do nothing on a ClientHello that has no usable boundary — the strategy proceeds with its other splits. Cost: at most one extra segment per matched connection.
fragmentation.middle_sni: When enabled, b4 cuts the TLS SNI field (the server hostname) itself across multiple TCP segments instead of leaving it intact in one segment. Useful against DPI engines that need the full SNI string in a single read to match a blocklist. Applies to several strategies that process the ClientHello (combo, oob, disorder, others) — not just combo. Has no effect on strategies that don't look at SNI (e.g. plain tcp/ip splits) and on ClientHellos where the SNI position can't be located. Cost: more segments per connection.
fragmentation.combo.decoy_enabled: Combo-only feature: before emitting the real fragmented ClientHello, b4 first sends a fake decoy packet to the destination using a payload from the set's faking configuration. Goal: make the first thing a DPI engine sees on this connection look uninteresting, so its inspection state is in the wrong place when the real ClientHello arrives. Cost: one extra packet plus the decoy payload's bandwidth per matched connection. Has no effect when the set's faking configuration produces no usable decoy payload, or when the active strategy is not combo.
fragmentation.combo.shuffle_mode: Controls the order in which combo emits its built-up segments. 'full' randomizes the entire emit order. 'reverse' sends segments in reverse (last first). 'middle' keeps the first and last segments in place and shuffles whatever's between them — only does something when there are more than three segments. TCP sequence numbers ensure the destination reassembles correctly regardless of emit order. Picking the right mode is ISP-dependent: some DPI engines refuse out-of-order segments and any non-natural mode wins; others reassemble fine and the choice has no effect.
fragmentation.combo.first_delay_ms: Milliseconds to wait after emitting the first segment of a combo split, before continuing with the rest. The min/max range form randomizes the wait per packet. Goal: create a clear time gap so the first segment looks like a complete read to DPI engines with short read windows. Cost: every emitted ClientHello adds this much to TLS-handshake latency. Setting both min and max to 0 does NOT disable the wait — it falls back to a strategy-internal default. Has no effect when the active strategy is not combo.
fragmentation.combo.jitter_max_us: Maximum microseconds of random gap b4 inserts between consecutive segments AFTER the first one (the first uses first_delay_ms instead). Each gap is rolled randomly between 0 and this value. Goal: break repeating-timing patterns that DPI could use to identify a known bypass tool. The min/max range form randomizes the cap itself per connection. Cost: small added latency per emitted segment. Setting both min and max to 0 falls back to a strategy-internal default rather than disabling. Has no effect when the active strategy is not combo.
fragmentation.combo.fake_per_segment: When enabled, b4 emits fake (overlap) packets before EACH segment of a combo split, not just before the first one. Requires fragmentation.seq_overlap_pattern to be configured — without a pattern, this toggle does nothing regardless of state. Each fake-emit produces fake_per_seg_count copies (default 1, hard max 11). Goal: confuse DPI's stream-reassembly state at every segment boundary, not just the start. Cost: more emitted packets per matched connection — bandwidth multiplier plus added CPU. Has no effect when the active strategy is not combo.
fragmentation.seq_overlap_pattern: List of bytes used as the fake payload b4 emits before each real ClientHello segment when the active strategy supports it (combo and disorder). The fake segment shares TCP sequence space with the real one that follows but carries this byte pattern instead. UI presets: 'none' (empty — disables the trick), 'tls12'/'tls11'/'tls10' (mimic the start of TLS records of those versions), 'http_get' (mimics the start of an HTTP GET), 'zeros' (a single null byte), 'custom' (user-specified bytes). Goal: give DPI engines a plausible-looking 'first read' so their stream-reassembly state is set up against the fake before the real ClientHello arrives. Empty pattern means fake_per_segment and the related fake-emit features have nothing to emit — they become no-ops. Picking the right preset is ISP-dependent: which one (if any) helps depends on what the local DPI engine does with overlapping segment data. Discovery doesn't tune this — manual selection or empty are the typical choices.
fragmentation.disorder.shuffle_mode: Disorder-specific. Controls the order in which the disorder strategy emits its segments. 'full' randomizes the entire emit order. 'reverse' sends segments in reverse (last first). TCP sequence numbers ensure correct reassembly at the destination regardless of emit order. Note: disorder, unlike combo, does NOT have a 'middle' mode. Picking the right mode is ISP-dependent: some DPI engines refuse out-of-order segments (any non-natural mode wins), others reassemble correctly (the choice has no effect). Has no effect when the active strategy is not disorder.
fragmentation.disorder.jitter: min_jitter_us and max_jitter_us together bound the random microsecond gap b4 inserts between consecutive segments under the disorder strategy. Each gap is rolled randomly in [min, max]. Goal: break repeating-timing patterns DPI could use to identify a known bypass tool. IMPORTANT interaction: this microsecond jitter is ONLY used when tcp.seg2delay is 0. If seg2delay is non-zero (millisecond delay set), seg2delay takes over and these jitter bounds are ignored entirely. When both fields are 0 or invalid, b4 falls back to a strategy-internal default rather than disabling. If max_jitter_us is set lower than min_jitter_us, max gets bumped up automatically — the configured min is treated as the floor.
fragmentation.disorder.fake_per_segment: When enabled (disorder strategy), b4 emits fake overlap packets before EACH segment of the disorder split, not just before the first one. Requires fragmentation.seq_overlap_pattern to be configured — without a pattern, this toggle does nothing regardless of state. Each fake-emit produces fake_per_seg_count copies (default 1, hard max 11). Goal: confuse DPI's stream-reassembly state at every segment boundary, not just the start. Cost: more emitted packets per matched connection — bandwidth multiplier and added CPU. Has no effect when the active strategy is not disorder.
